content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive Legal · · Enforcement · · Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-2901           322-6441              322-6444

September 23, 1983

SUPERSEDED

Rich Eichtman
Nielsen, Hodgson, Parrinello
and Mueller
1225 - 8th Street, Ste. 550
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: FPPC No. A-83-210

Dear Rich:

     This letter is in reply to your request for advice regarding the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act. You have asked for clarification of the advice provided to you in the letter dated July 1, 1977, from Alan Herndon. In that letter the reporting obligations of a lobbyist, and the lobbyist gift prohibition, were addressed in connection with the following situation:

     A lobbyist and a legislator have dinner together. The lobbyist charges the dinner on his or her credit card and the legislator reimburses the lobbyist for his or her portion of the dinner at a later date.

     This letter will clarify the reporting obligations, and the gift prohibition, as they apply to the above situation.

     With respect to reporting, Gov. Code Section 86107 requires that a lobbyist report all payments made -- or, in the case of activity expenses, incurred -- during the period covered by the report. However, if a lobbyist makes a payment which benefits an official, and the official reimburses the lobbyist before the end of the calendar month, the lobbyist has no obligation to report that portion which has been reimbursed. Or, in the case of a payment made less than one week prior to the end of the month, if reimbursement is received within one week of making the payment, the lobbyist need not report it.

     If reimbursement is not received within the times indicated above, the lobbyist must report that portion of the payment which benefited the official as a note to Part III, Section A of the Form 620, with a notation that either (1) reimbursement has been requested but not yet received; or (2) reimbursement has been received. (The amount which is to be reimbursed should not be added in to the "Amount" column.)

Rich Eichman
September 23, 1983
Page 2


     In all cases, if the amount being reimbursed is $1.00 or
more, reimbursement must be made by check, and a copy of the
check must be kept with the lobbyist's records.

     With respect to the gift prohibition contained in Gov. Code
Sections 86203 and 86204, I would like to point out that in
order to avoid the appearance of an improper gift, lobbyists and
officials should make every effort to avoid a situation in which
a lobbyist makes a payment of more than $10 which benefits an
official. We recognize that there may be situations in which it
may be unavoidable, and we provide the following advice for those
situations. If a lobbyist makes a payment of more than $10 which
benefits an official, no violation of the gift prohibitions will
occur so long as reimbursement is received pursuant to the same
conditions indicated above for reporting, i.e., the lobbyist is
reimbursed by the official before the end of the calendar month
in which the payment was made. The exception to this is if the
payment is made less than a week prior to the end of the month,
no violation will occur so long as the lobbyist receives reimbursement within one week of making the payment.

     Thank you for your inquiry. Please call me if you have
any questions.

                                             Sincerely,

                                             [signature]
                                             Jeanne Pritchard
                                             Political Reform Consultant

JP:kt
cc: Sunny Jung
    Franchise Tax Board

sections.question: A lobbyist and a legislator have come together. The lobbyist charges the dinner on his or her credit card and the legislator reimburses the lobbyist for his or her portion of the dinner at a later date. What are the reporting obligations of a lobbyist, and does the lobbyist gift prohibition apply to this situation?
sections.conclusion: With respect to reporting, if a lobbyist makes a payment which benefits an official and the official reimburses the lobbyist before the end of the calendar month, the lobbyist has no obligation to report that portion which has been reimbursed. With respect to the gift prohibition, no violation will occur so long as reimbursement is received before the end of the calendar month in which the payment was made (or within one week if payment made less than one week before month-end).
sections.question_synthetic: What are the reporting obligations and gift prohibition implications when a lobbyist pays for a meal that benefits a legislator and is subsequently reimbursed?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A lobbyist need not report reimbursed meal expenses if the official reimburses before the end of the calendar month, and the gift prohibition is not violated if reimbursement occurs within the specified timeframes. Payments of $10 or more must be tracked and reimbursed by check if the amount is $10 or more.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is an FPPC response dated September 23, 1983 (No. A-83-210). Contains clear question and conclusion sections regarding Government Code Sections 86107, 86203, and 86204. Slight OCR artifacts present but content is clearly legible and complete.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A lobbyist and a legislator have come together. The lobbyist charges the dinner on his or her credit card and the legislator reimburses the lobbyist for his or her portion of the dinner at a later date. What are the reporting obligations of a lobbyist, and does the lobbyist gift prohibition apply to this situation?

CONCLUSION: With respect to reporting, if a lobbyist makes a payment which benefits an official and the official reimburses the lobbyist before the end of the calendar month, the lobbyist has no obligation to report that portion which has been reimbursed. With respect to the gift prohibition, no violation will occur so long as reimbursement is received before the end of the calendar month in which the payment was made (or within one week if payment made less than one week before month-end).

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter clarifying reporting obligations and gift prohibition rules for lobbyists who pay for meals that benefit legislators and receive reimbursement. The letter establishes that timely reimbursement (by month-end or within one week of payment) avoids reporting requirements and gift prohibition violations.
